statement.markdown: Given an array of integers containing duplicate elements and their frequencies, find the length of the shortest subarray that covers all the integers. The subarray should cover each integer at least once according to their given frequencies.
statement.inputFormat:
Input contains two lines: the length of the array and a line with elements and their frequencies (space separated) e.g. 7 
3 4 2 5 2 1 1
statement.outputFormat: Return the length of the shortest subarray
statement.notes: This is a Two Pointers problem. You can use the Sliding Window technique to find the shortest subarray.

hints.text: Use a two-pointer technique to find the shortest subarray.

editorial.title: Two Pointers Technique for Shortest Subarray
editorial.intuition: The idea is to use two pointers to find the shortest subarray. The left pointer is used to track the starting position of the subarray, and the right pointer is used to track the ending position.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach would involve checking all possible subarrays and finding the shortest one. This would involve a time complexity of O(n^2).
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a two-pointer technique to find the shortest subarray in O(n) time complexity.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a two-pointer technique and a HashMap to store the frequency of each element.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proved by considering the time complexity.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run will help in understanding the correct approach.
commonMistakes: Mistaking the two-pointer technique with the Sliding Window technique
Not using a HashMap to store the frequency of each element
